content.text: The Measurement Problem in AI Training

Most enterprise training programmes measure Level 1 (did learners like the training?) and Level 2 (did learners pass the assessment?) and stop there. For Claude training, this is especially dangerous because a learner who scores well on a knowledge quiz about acceptable use may still default to risky prompting behaviour when under time pressure in their real job. The gap between what learners know in a test environment and what they do in their daily workflow is the measurement gap that matters most for AI adoption.

Effective measurement of Claude training effectiveness requires data from four distinct sources: assessment scores from the training system, usage telemetry from the Claude deployment, work quality signals from downstream business processes, and qualitative feedback from managers and employees. No single source gives a complete picture.

attrs.alt: Kirkpatrick Four-Level Model — Claude Training Measurement

content.text: Reaction surveys measure how learners perceived the training — whether it was relevant, engaging, and useful. While reaction data alone cannot tell you whether behaviour changed, strongly negative reaction scores are leading indicators of low transfer: a learner who found the training irrelevant to their actual job is unlikely to apply what they learned.

from anthropic import Anthropic
import json

client = Anthropic()

def analyse_reaction_survey_responses(
    role: str,
    module_name: str,
    survey_responses: list[dict],
) -> dict:
    """Analyse open-text reaction survey responses to identify curriculum improvement needs."""
    responses_text = "\n".join(
        f"- Usefulness: {r.get('usefulness', 'N/A')}/5 | "
        f"Relevance: {r.get('relevance', 'N/A')}/5 | "
        f"Comment: {r.get('comment', '')}"
        for r in survey_responses[:50]  # cap for token economy
    )
    prompt = f"""Analyse reaction survey responses for Claude training.

Role: {role}
Module: {module_name}
Responses ({len(survey_responses)} total, showing first 50):
{responses_text}

Identify:
1. mean_usefulness: average (1 decimal)
2. mean_relevance: average (1 decimal)
3. top_themes_positive: list of 3 recurring positive themes
4. top_themes_negative: list of 3 recurring concerns or complaints
5. curriculum_changes: list of 2–3 specific, actionable improvements based on feedback
6. flag_for_review: true/false — flag if mean scores < 3.5 or any systemic concern

Return JSON only."""

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return json.loads(resp.content[0].text)


analysis = analyse_reaction_survey_responses(
    role="Customer Support Agent",
    module_name="Drafting Support Emails with Claude",
    survey_responses=[
        {"usefulness": 4, "relevance": 5, "comment": "The ticket examples were exactly what we see every day"},
        {"usefulness": 2, "relevance": 3, "comment": "Too much theory at the start, wanted to try it sooner"},
        {"usefulness": 4, "relevance": 4, "comment": "Good but needed more examples of what NOT to send to Claude"},
        {"usefulness": 3, "relevance": 4, "comment": "The quiz was easy — practical part was harder and more useful"},
    ],
)
print(f"Usefulness: {analysis['mean_usefulness']} | Relevance: {analysis['mean_relevance']}")
print(f"Flag for review: {analysis['flag_for_review']}")
for change in analysis['curriculum_changes']:
    print(f"  Recommended change: {change}")
Negative theme in > 20% of comments triggers module owner review

Assessment scores measure whether learners acquired the knowledge and skills the curriculum intended to teach. For Claude training, two assessment types provide complementary evidence: knowledge quizzes (multiple-choice, scenario-based) test declarative understanding of acceptable use, data policy, and prompting principles; practical assessments test the procedural skill of actually using Claude to produce work of acceptable quality.

Behaviour measurement answers the question: did the training change what employees actually do? For Claude training, this means tracking whether certified employees use Claude in their work, how frequently they use it, and whether their usage aligns with the approved use cases they were trained on. Usage analytics from the Claude deployment are the primary data source; manager observation and work-sample review provide qualitative depth.
Active user rate (% of certified users active in last 7 days)
< 40% at day 30 = significant adoption barrier; investigate blockers
Sessions per active user per day
< 1 session/day suggests Claude is not yet habitual; 3–5 is healthy
Mismatch between trained use cases and actual prompts = curriculum gap
< 3.0 triggers 1:1 between change lead and manager
Systematic quality issues trigger remedial session for affected cohort

def generate_behaviour_transfer_report(
    role: str,
    cohort_name: str,
    certification_date: str,
    days_since_certification: int,
    usage_stats: dict,
    manager_survey_data: dict,
) -> str:
    """Generate a behaviour transfer assessment report for a certified cohort."""
    prompt = f"""Write a behaviour transfer report for Claude-certified employees.

Role: {role}
Cohort: {cohort_name}
Certified: {certification_date} ({days_since_certification} days ago)

Usage data:
- Active users this week: {usage_stats.get("active_pct", 0)}% of certified
- Sessions per active user per day: {usage_stats.get("avg_daily_sessions", 0)}
- Top use case: {usage_stats.get("top_use_case", "N/A")}
- % using only trained use cases: {usage_stats.get("in_scope_pct", 0)}%
- % attempting out-of-scope prompts: {usage_stats.get("out_of_scope_pct", 0)}%

Manager survey ({manager_survey_data.get("response_rate_pct", 0)}% response rate):
- Mean transfer score: {manager_survey_data.get("mean_transfer_score", 0)}/5
- % reporting "visible daily use": {manager_survey_data.get("daily_use_pct", 0)}%
- Top reported barrier: {manager_survey_data.get("top_barrier", "N/A")}

Write a 3-paragraph report covering:
1. Transfer status (is the training sticking? what does the data show?)
2. Top two risks or concerns
3. Recommended actions for the next 30 days, with owners"""

    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=500,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


report = generate_behaviour_transfer_report(
    role="Support Agent",
    cohort_name="Wave 1 APAC",
    certification_date="2025-02-10",
    days_since_certification=30,
    usage_stats={"active_pct": 58, "avg_daily_sessions": 2.8, "top_use_case": "email drafting", "in_scope_pct": 91, "out_of_scope_pct": 9},
    manager_survey_data={"response_rate_pct": 72, "mean_transfer_score": 3.4, "daily_use_pct": 55, "top_barrier": "Not enough time to learn during shift"},
)
print(report)

Level 4 measures whether the training produced tangible business outcomes. For Claude adoption, typical results metrics include: reduction in average ticket handle time, improvement in first-response quality scores, reduction in escalation rates, and increase in knowledge base utilisation. Attributing business outcomes to training (rather than other factors) requires a comparison group or a before-and-after analysis with controls — a pure post-training measurement cannot isolate training as the cause.

def calculate_training_roi(
    cohort_size: int,
    training_cost_per_person: float,
    productivity_gain_minutes_per_day: float,
    working_days_in_period: int,
    hourly_cost_per_person: float,
    error_reduction_incidents: int,
    average_incident_resolution_cost: float,
) -> dict:
    """Calculate a simplified ROI for Claude training investment."""
    total_training_cost = cohort_size * training_cost_per_person
    
    # Productivity benefit
    daily_saving_per_person = (productivity_gain_minutes_per_day / 60) * hourly_cost_per_person
    total_productivity_benefit = daily_saving_per_person * cohort_size * working_days_in_period
    
    # Error reduction benefit
    total_error_benefit = error_reduction_incidents * average_incident_resolution_cost
    
    total_benefit = total_productivity_benefit + total_error_benefit
    net_benefit = total_benefit - total_training_cost
    roi_pct = (net_benefit / total_training_cost) * 100 if total_training_cost > 0 else 0
    payback_days = (total_training_cost / (total_productivity_benefit / working_days_in_period)) if total_productivity_benefit > 0 else float("inf")
    
    return {
        "total_training_cost": round(total_training_cost, 2),
        "total_productivity_benefit": round(total_productivity_benefit, 2),
        "total_error_reduction_benefit": round(total_error_benefit, 2),
        "total_benefit": round(total_benefit, 2),
        "net_benefit": round(net_benefit, 2),
        "roi_pct": round(roi_pct, 1),
        "payback_days": round(payback_days, 0),
    }


roi = calculate_training_roi(
    cohort_size=120,
    training_cost_per_person=450,       # design + delivery + LMS + facilitator time
    productivity_gain_minutes_per_day=18,  # measured via time-tracking comparison
    working_days_in_period=60,
    hourly_cost_per_person=35,
    error_reduction_incidents=8,        # P3 incidents avoided in 60-day period
    average_incident_resolution_cost=1200,
)
print(f"Training cost: £{roi['total_training_cost']:,.0f}")
print(f"Productivity benefit: £{roi['total_productivity_benefit']:,.0f}")
print(f"Error reduction benefit: £{roi['total_error_reduction_benefit']:,.0f}")
print(f"ROI: {roi['roi_pct']}% | Payback: {roi['payback_days']:.0f} days")

Measurement data has no value unless it drives curriculum changes. Establish a quarterly curriculum review cycle where the L&D owner, change lead, and a subject-matter expert review all four levels of data and produce a prioritised list of curriculum changes for the next cohort. The review should answer three questions: what is working well that we should preserve or extend? What is not working that we should change? What is missing that we have not yet taught?
— focus on Level 1 and 2: are learners engaging and learning? Common actions: shorten theoretical sections, add more role-specific examples, adjust quiz difficulty calibration.
— focus on Level 3: is training transferring? Common actions: add just-in-time reference cards, improve manager coaching guides, adjust certification renewal triggers.

— focus on Level 4: are business results improving? Common actions: add advanced use-case modules for high-performing cohorts, create peer-led learning programmes, develop Tier 2 and 3 certification tracks.
The curriculum that ships to Wave 1 should not be the same curriculum that ships to Wave 4. Measurement data from early cohorts is the most valuable curriculum improvement input you will ever have.

A single view that consolidates all four Kirkpatrick levels removes the friction of pulling data from multiple systems before each review meeting. The dashboard does not need to be elaborate — a well-structured spreadsheet updated weekly by the change lead serves the purpose for most enterprises. Automated dashboards connected to the LMS, usage analytics, and survey tools become worthwhile once the programme scales beyond 500 learners.

def generate_dashboard_summary(
    period_label: str,
    reaction_data: dict,
    learning_data: dict,
    behaviour_data: dict,
    results_data: dict,
) -> str:
    """Generate a weekly measurement dashboard summary for stakeholders."""
    prompt = f"""Write a one-page training effectiveness dashboard summary.

Period: {period_label}

Level 1 — Reaction:
- Module usefulness mean: {reaction_data.get("usefulness_mean", "N/A")}/5
- Role relevance mean: {reaction_data.get("relevance_mean", "N/A")}/5
- Training NPS: {reaction_data.get("nps", "N/A")}
- Top negative theme: {reaction_data.get("top_issue", "N/A")}

Level 2 — Learning:
- Certifications issued this period: {learning_data.get("certs_issued", 0)}
- Cohort mean assessment score: {learning_data.get("mean_score", "N/A")}%
- Pass rate: {learning_data.get("pass_rate_pct", "N/A")}%
- Questions with < 60% correct rate: {learning_data.get("weak_questions_count", 0)}

Level 3 — Behaviour (30 days post-cert):
- Active user rate: {behaviour_data.get("active_pct", "N/A")}%
- Avg daily sessions per active user: {behaviour_data.get("avg_daily_sessions", "N/A")}
- Manager transfer score: {behaviour_data.get("manager_score", "N/A")}/5
- Out-of-scope prompt rate: {behaviour_data.get("out_of_scope_pct", "N/A")}%

Level 4 — Results:
- Productivity gain (vs baseline): {results_data.get("productivity_gain_pct", "N/A")}%
- Error rate change: {results_data.get("error_rate_change", "N/A")}%
- Training-linked incidents this period: {results_data.get("incidents", 0)}

Write:
1. RAG status for each level (Red / Amber / Green with one-line rationale)
2. Two recommended actions for the next 14 days
3. One metric to watch closely next period"""

    from anthropic import Anthropic
    client = Anthropic()
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=600,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text


summary = generate_dashboard_summary(
    period_label="Week ending 2025-03-14 — Wave 1 APAC Support",
    reaction_data={"usefulness_mean": 3.8, "relevance_mean": 4.1, "nps": 28, "top_issue": "Not enough practice time in labs"},
    learning_data={"certs_issued": 34, "mean_score": 84, "pass_rate_pct": 82, "weak_questions_count": 2},
    behaviour_data={"active_pct": 58, "avg_daily_sessions": 2.8, "manager_score": 3.4, "out_of_scope_pct": 9},
    results_data={"productivity_gain_pct": 12, "error_rate_change": -8, "incidents": 1},
)
print(summary)

— '94% of employees completed the training' is a delivery metric, not an effectiveness metric. Completion proves that employees clicked through content; it proves nothing about what they learned or changed.

— a post-module survey after every single module in a six-module curriculum produces eight surveys for a learner. Consolidate: one brief mid-track survey and one end-of-track survey is sufficient for Levels 1 and 2.
Attributing all productivity change to training

— if the organisation also changed its support policies, hired additional staff, and upgraded its ticketing system in the same quarter, attributing all productivity improvement to Claude training is not credible. Control for confounding variables, or at minimum acknowledge them in reports.

— business results from a training programme take 60–90 days to materialise. Reporting Level 4 metrics at week two post-training will show nothing meaningful and may lead to premature conclusions about programme failure.
No feedback loop to curriculum designers

— measurement data collected by the analytics team but never shared with the L&D team who designed the curriculum is wasted effort. Establish a formal handoff: analytics team produces the data; L&D team interprets it and produces curriculum actions; change lead tracks whether actions were implemented.
Not every metric requires a curriculum review. Define escalation thresholds in advance so the measurement process is triggered by data, not by whoever is most vocal in a given week.
3+ per cohort per quarter (full curriculum audit)
Publish these thresholds to your programme steering committee before the first cohort completes training. Pre-agreed thresholds remove politics from what should be a data-driven decision.

Training effectiveness measurement for Claude adoption requires four levels of evidence: reaction (did learners value it?), learning (did they acquire skills?), behaviour (do they apply them?), and results (did business outcomes improve?). Claude can assist with survey analysis, assessment grading, cohort performance summaries, and ROI calculation — but the decisions about what to change and why must rest with the humans who own the training programme. Measurement without action is data collection; measurement with a quarterly iteration cycle is continuous improvement.
